coredata.dc:title: Integration of β-cyclodextrin into graphene quantum dot nano-structure and its application towards detection of Vitamin C at physiological pH: A new electrochemical approach
coredata.dc:creator: Shadjou, Nasrin
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Talebi, Faeze
Marjani, Ahmad Poursattar
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) attachment to graphene quantum dot (GQD) structure was performed using simultaneous electrodeposition of GQD and β-CD on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Cyclic voltammetry at potential range −1.0 to 1.0V from mixture of GQD and β-CD produced a well-defined β-CD-GQD deposited on the surface of glassy carbon electrode. β-CD-GQD modified GCE was used as a new electrocatalytical nanocomposite towards electrooxidation of Vitamin C (as sample analyte). The synergistic effects and the catalytic activity of the β-CD-GQD modified GCE were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) chronoamperometry (CA) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The process of oxidation involved and its kinetics were established by using cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry techniques. It has been found that in the course of an anodic potential sweep the electro-oxidation of Vitamin C is catalyzed by synergetic effect of β-CD and GQD through a mediated electron transfer mechanism. Therefore, β-CD-GQDs promote the rate of oxidation by increasing the peak current. The cyclic voltammetric results indicate that β-CD-GQDs-GCE can remarkably enhance electroactivity towards the oxidation of Vitamin C in buffer solution. We have illustrated that the as-obtained β-CD-GQDs-GCE exhibited a much higher electrocatalytical behavior than GQDs for the electrooxidation and detection of Vitamin C which was about two fold higher than for GQDs. The electrochemical behavior was further exploited as detection scheme for the Vitamin C electrooxidation by square wave voltammetry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocalyst
β-cyclodextrin
Graphene quantum dot
Vitamin C
Nano-composite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs and β-CD-GQDs modified GCE
Electrochemical detection of uric acid (UA) at GQD-β-CD-GCE
Electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA) at GQD-β-CD-GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) TEM and AFM image, (C, D) fluorescence spectra and DLS analysis of GQD.
CVs of bare GCE (A) and β-CD/GQDs/GCE (B) in solution without and with 0.01mM Vitamin C. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1M; PBS (pH=7.4); scan rate: 100mVs−1.
CVs of 10μM Vitamin C at GCE, GQDs/GCE, and β-CD/ GQDs/GCE. Supporting electrolyte: 0.1M; PBS (pH=7.4); scan rate: 100mVs−1.

CVs of β-CD/GQDs/GCE in the presence of 0.01mM Vitamin C+0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) in different scan rates (from inner to outer): 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 90, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 50, 600, 700, 800, 900, and 1000mVs−1, respectively. Inset: peak current vs. scan rate.
Current function vs. ν 0.5 for 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) in the presence of 1mM Vitamin C.

Chronoamperograms of β-CD/GQDs/GCE in 0.1M PBS in the absence (A) and presence (B–F) of various concentrations of Vitamin C: 0, 0.001, 0.0014, 0.0016, 0.002, 0.0025, 0.005, and 0.01mM, respectively. Insets: Variation of chronoamperometry currents at vs. time−1/2. Anodic currents obtained for a potential step of 0.15V.

(A) CVs of GQD-GCE (curve a) and GQD-β-CD-GCE (curve b) in the presence of 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4)+(0.0025g/mL) uric acid (A). (B) CVs of GQD-GCE (curve a) and GQD-β-CD-GCE (curve b) in the presence of 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4)+(0.0025g/mL) dopamine. Scan rate: 100mVs−1.

SWVs of β-CD/GQDs/GCE in different concentrations of Vitamin C solutions (0.0007, 0.0005, 0.0003, 0.0002, 0.0001, 0.009, 0.006, 0.004, 0.002, 0.001, 0.09, 0.06, 0.004, 0.002 and 0.01mM) Supporting electrolyte: 0.1M; PBS (pH=7.4); scan rate: 50mVs−1.
Schematic setup for the synthesis of GQD and its electrodeposition on GCE electrode using electrochemical methods.
The electrochemical figures of merit exhibited by GQD-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE.
Comparison relationship between the electrochemical figures of merit exhibited by GQD-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE and functional groups of selected analytes.
Analytical parameters determined for calibration curves of Vitamin C on GQD-β-CD-GCE.
Comparison of the proposed electrochemical sensor for determination of Vitamin C with some of other electrochemical sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Inter-day precision, accuracy and recoveries of the method for assay of Vitamin C.
Intra-day precision, accuracy and recoveries for determination of Vitamin C.

head.ce:title: Integration of β-cyclodextrin into graphene quantum dot nano-structure and its application towards detection of Vitamin C at physiological pH: A new electrochemical approach

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) attachment to graphene quantum dot (GQD) structure was performed using simultaneous electrodeposition of GQD and β-CD on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Cyclic voltammetry at potential range −1.0 to 1.0V from mixture of GQD and β-CD produced a well-defined β-CD-GQD deposited on the surface of glassy carbon electrode. β-CD-GQD modified GCE was used as a new electrocatalytical nanocomposite towards electrooxidation of Vitamin C (as sample analyte). The synergistic effects and the catalytic activity of the β-CD-GQD modified GCE were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) chronoamperometry (CA) and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The process of oxidation involved and its kinetics were established by using cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry techniques. It has been found that in the course of an anodic potential sweep the electro-oxidation of Vitamin C is catalyzed by synergetic effect of β-CD and GQD through a mediated electron transfer mechanism. Therefore, β-CD-GQDs promote the rate of oxidation by increasing the peak current. The cyclic voltammetric results indicate that β-CD-GQDs-GCE can remarkably enhance electroactivity towards the oxidation of Vitamin C in buffer solution. We have illustrated that the as-obtained β-CD-GQDs-GCE exhibited a much higher electrocatalytical behavior than GQDs for the electrooxidation and detection of Vitamin C which was about two fold higher than for GQDs. The electrochemical behavior was further exploited as detection scheme for the Vitamin C electrooxidation by square wave voltammetry.

Stable electroactive modified electrode using electrodeposition of GQD in the presence β-CD was prepared by cyclic voltammetry. It was found that the β-CD-GQD-GCE exhibit good catalytic activity for the oxidation of Vitamin C at a reduced overpotential and it has a significant high response for Vitamin C oxidation.
Image 4

ce:section.ce:para: CDs are a well-known class of natural host molecules obtained from the enzymatic degradation of starch [1–5]. CDs with their largely hydrophobic cavities of variable size and numerous ways of chemical modification are the subject of intensive electrochemical research including both their behavior in homogeneous solutions and in thin films attached to the electrode surfaces [6–8]. Electrocatalytical methods measuring the current response to the potential applied, linear scan, staircase, pulse voltammetries, and potential-step techniques such as chronoamperometry and chronocoulometry, allow to trigger or to monitor changes in the redox states of electroactive analytes entrapped on the sites in CD-based systems. Linear scan (LSV) or staircase (SCV) cyclic voltammetries (CV) are the most frequently employed electrochemical techniques for these studies. They provide useful information on the nature of the reduced and oxidized forms of the electroactive compounds such as analytes, and on the mechanistic aspects of the electrode processes. They also allow monitoring even very subtle changes of the molecular environment of the redox centers by following their redox potentials. Therefore, CDs are employed in electrochemical sensing devices for the determination of selected analytes.

One of the most important characteristics of CDs is the formation of inclusion complexes with various organic and inorganic guest molecules [9–12]. Upon inclusion complexation, the characteristic properties of the guest molecule inside the CD cavities, such as solubility, redox and spectral will be changed significantly. This unique property has led to widespread utilization of CDs in pharmaceutical, chemical and other industrial areas. The stability of the inclusion complexes depends on the size and the polarity of the guest molecules, the nature of the medium, and the temperature. Based on size selective complexation properties of CDs, the better the fit of guest molecules inside the cavity of CD, the more stable the complex will be produced. The stability of the complex is proportional to the polarity of the guest molecule. Vitamin C is a polar analyte therefore entrap of this analyte on CD can be survey. Vitamin C is an electroactive substance. However, its electrochemical signal cannot notably enhance when Vitamin C molecule entered into the film containing β-CD at the β-CD modified electrodes. The signal enhancement can be done by secondary materials such as Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [13], graphene [13] and so on. Compared with β-CD/MWCNTs modified electrode, β-CD/graphene hybrid films showed most attractive analytical performance, as clearly illustrated from the response signal [12–14].

These improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of β-CD/graphene or its conductive derivatives such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and functionalized GQDs. GQDs increase the contact area with the analyte, so they could increase the electrochemical active surface to interact with some electroactive analytes [15]. Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrocatalysis, therefore modification of different substrates (such as glass, carbon, graphite etc.) by GQDs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of β-CD into GQDs can be provide by the zero-dimensional structure of the deposited films and greatly increases Faradic currents. Thus, in the present work, we used β-CD-GQDs as modifier material and studied the electrochemical oxidation of Vitamin C at β-CD-GQDs modified glassy carbon electrode and developing a new electrocalyst for electrooxidation and of Vitamin C. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the electrocalytical oxidation of Vitamin C based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on GQDs or β-CD-GQDs.

All chemicals were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and used without further purification. Alumina slurry was purchased from Beuhler (Illinois, USA) and raw material of Vitamin C was purchased from Merck (Germany). All solutions were prepared with deionized water. The stock solution of Vitamin C (0.001g per mL) was prepared by dissolving an accurate amount of Vitamin C in an appropriate volume of 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (PBS), pH=7.4 (which was also used as supporting electrolyte), and then stored in the dark place at 4°C. Additional dilute solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. Also the other stock solutions were prepared by dissolving an accurate amount equal to molecular weight of each one in an appropriate volume of 1000mL deionized water and then all stored in the dark place at 4°C.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a three-electrode cell setup using PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a Personal Computer using NOVA1.7 software. Saturated Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode and the counter electrode (also known as auxiliary electrode), which usually made of an inert material was platinum. All potentials were measured with respect to the Ag/AgCl which was positioned as close to the working electrode as possible by means of a luggin capillary. Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (from Azar electrode Co., Urmia, Iran) was used as the working electrode. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on Leo 906, Zeiss, (Germany). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) experiments were performed at contact mode by Nanowizard AFM (JPK Instruments AG, Berlin, Germany) mounted on Olympus Invert Microscope IX81 (Olympus Co., Tokyo, Japan). UV–Vis spectroscopy was performed by Cecil, Cambridge, (UK). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using Siemens, D500, (Germany). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) were obtained using Malvern 3500 ZS. Spectrofluorimetery test was performed using Jasco, FP-750, (Tokyo, Japan).

An easy bottom-up method was used for the preparation of GQDs. At first, GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions [16]. Briefly, 2g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100mL of 10mg/mL NaOH solution was added into the orange homogenous liquid dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month at 4°C (See Scheme 1 ).

Fig. 1 (A and B) presents the AFM and TEM images of synthesized GQDs. The corresponding AFM image shows a single GQD monolayer thin film. Ninety percent of the particles represent dark brown color which assigned to a size range below 10nm. Furthermore, the DLS study represented hydrodynamic sizes of GQDs with size distribution of 5±4nm (Fig. 1C) which findings of DLS analysis confirmed the AFM results.

Fig. 1D shows fluorescence spectra of the GQD dispersed in water at room temperature. The GQDs have a broad absorption, from 400 to 600nm. The maximum excitation of 210nm was obtained with an emission wavelength of ~470nm. The maximum emission of ~480nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 400nm. Also when the excitation wavelength changed from 340 to 420nm, the maximum peaks were constant. This could be explained by the uniformity both in the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQD which was responsible for the fluorescence of GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs and β-CD-GQDs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: GCE (2mm in diameter) was polished to a mirror-like finish with 0.3 and 0.05μm alumina slurry and then thoroughly rinsed with double distilled water. Then it was successively sonicated in acetone and double distilled water and was allowed to dry at room temperature. Finally, 5mL homogenous GQD and β-CD-GQDs films were electrodeposited onto GCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from −1.0 to 1.0V at a scan rate of 200mVs−1 for 50cycles. After 50 repeating cycles, the three-electrode system was transferred into 0.1M phosphate buffer solution (pH=7.4) containing Vitamin C and cyclic voltammograms were recorded at a sweep rate of 100mVs−1.

The voltammograms of Vitamin C at a bare GCE, and β-CD-GQDs/GCE in PBS (pH6.0) were shown in Fig. 2 . As seen in Fig. 2A any oxidation signal was not exhibited on the GCE. This indicated the electro-inactivity of Vitamin C on the GC surface. Typical cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of β-β-CD-GQDs/GCE in 0.1M PBS is shown as Fig. 2B where potential sweep rate of 100mVs−1 has been employed. In the absence of Vitamin C, no peak appears at the β-CD-GQDs/GCE. While Vitamin C exhibits one oxidation peak, located at 0.98mV. It showed that no reduction peak was observed in the reverse scan, suggesting that the electrochemical reaction was a totally irreversible process. As can be seen, the anodic peak potential for the oxidation of Vitamin Cat β-CD-GQDs/GCE is about 0.98V. The oxidation peak current value (Ip) of Vitamin C was 23μA. The data obtained clearly show that the combination of GQDs and β-CD definitely improve the characteristics of Vitamin Coxidation. That might be related to the excellent property of β-CD-GQDs, such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. The role of β-CD was also important for promoting the electrochemical oxidation of Vitamin C. So β-CD-GQDs could be substituted for the oxidation of Vitamin C. Fig. 3 shows the CV of 0.01mM Vitamin C at GCE, GQDs/GCE, and β-CD-GQDs-GCE. The current response of Vitamin C at the β-CD-GQDs-GCE is much higher than that at the GCE, GQDs/GCE, indicating that β-CD-GQDs-GCE possesses remarkable enhancement effects on the oxidation of Vitamin C. It is noteworthy that the present response of Vitamin C was dramatically improved when β-CD was introduced to the surface of GQDs-GCE. This can attribute to hydrogen bonding between the β-CD and Vitamin C. This leads to a greater amount of Vitamin C accumulation on the surface of β-CD-GQDs-GCE. Therefore Vitamin C could be more accumulated on the electrode surface due to their interaction with β-CD. Therefore, for these two modified electrodes the value of the oxidation peak current follows the order of Ip [β-CD-GQDs-GCE]>Ip [GQDs-GCE]>Ip [GCE].

Useful information involving electrochemical mechanism usually can be acquired from the relationship between peak current and scan rate. Therefore, the electrochemical behaviors of Vitamin C at different scan rates were investigated on the surface of β-CD/GQDs/GCE by CV. A linear relationship was obtained between the peak current and the scan rate in the range of 2–1000mVs−1, which revealed that the oxidation of Vitamin C was a diffusion-controlled step (Fig. 4 ).

The peak potential shifted to positive values with the increasing scan rates. The linear relation between peak potential and logarithm of scan rate can be expressed as Ep =0.0218ln v +0.0902 (R2 =9854). As for an irreversible electrode process, according to Vitamin C [17], Ep is defined by the following equation:
(1)
E p
=
E 0
+
R T
1 − α
n F
ln v
where Ep is the peak potential (V vs. Ag/AgCl), E 0 is the formal potential (V vs. Ag/AgCl), R is the universal gas constant (8.314JK−1 mol−1), T is the temperature (K), α is the charge transfer coefficient for the oxidation step, n is the number of electrons involved in the rate determining step, F is the Faraday constant (96,485°Cmol−1). According to Bard and Faulkner [18], for a totally irreversible wave, Ep is a function of scan rate, shifting (for an oxidation) in a positive direction by an amount 1.15RT/αF (or 30/α mV at 25°C) for each ten fold increase in v. So, from this we got the value of α to be 0.45. The number of electron (n) transferred in the electrooxidation of Vitamin C was calculated to be 2. In addition, with increasing scan rates, the peak separations began to increase, indicating that the limitation arose from charge transfer kinetics. Based on Laviron theory, the electron transfer rate constant (K
s ) and charge transfer coefficient (α) could be determined by measuring the variation of peak potential with scan rate. The peak potential values were proportional to the logarithm of the scan rate for the rates higher than 100mVs−1 (Fig. 4C). The calculated values for (K
s ) and (α) were approximately 9.0s−1 and 0.49, respectively. The surface concentration of electroactive species (Γ) can be calculated from the slope of the plot of Ipa verses scan rate and is about 1.44×10−9 molcm2.

Also, plotting the current function (peak current divided by the square root of the potential sweep rate) against the square root of the potential sweep rate (Fig. 5 ) revealed negative slope confirming the electrocatalytic nature of the process.

Literature information reveals that the increased anodic signal may also be a result of its inclusion into the hydrophobic cavity of β-CD [19]. This inclusion may result in a sort of pre-concentration of the analyte at the electrode surface, which will govern the kinetics of its charge transfer process. In the case of Vitamin C, the obtained slight increase in the redox peak current can be due to a partial inclusion into the β-CD.

In order to evaluate the reaction kinetics, the electrocatalytical oxidation of Vitamin C on β-CD/GQDs/GCE was investigated by chronoamperometry. Chronoamperometry, as well as cyclic voltammetry has been employed for the investigation of the processes occurring via an ErCi mechanism [20]. Double steps chronoamperograms were recorded by setting the working electrode potentials to desired values and were used to measure the catalytic rate constant on the modified surface. Fig. 6A shows double steps chronoamperograms for the modified electrode in the absence (a) and presence (b: 0.001, 0.0014, 0.0016, 0.002, 0.0025, 0.005, and 0.01mM) of Vitamin C over a concentration range of 0.001–0.01mM. The applied potential steps were 0.15 vs. (Ag/AgCl)/V, respectively. The plot of net current versus t −1/2 which has been obtained by removing the background current by the point-by-point subtraction method gives a straight line, Fig. 6B. This indicates that the transient current must be controlled by a diffusion process. The transient current is due to catalytic oxidation of Vitamin C which increases as the Vitamin C concentration is raised. By using the slopes of these lines; we can obtain the diffusion coefficients of the drugs according to the Cottrell equation [20,p. 163]:
(2)
I = nFA
D
1 / 2
C *
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2
where D is the diffusion coefficient, and C ⁎ is the bulk concentration. The mean value of the diffusion coefficients of Vitamin C was found to be 9×10−6 cm2 s−1.

The rate constants of the reactions of Vitamin C and the ensuing intermediates with the redox sites of the β-CD/GQDs/GCE electrode can be derived from the chronoamperograms according to Eq. (3) [20,p. 503]:
(3)
I catal
I d
=
λ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
erf
λ
1 / 2
+
exp
− λ
λ
1 / 2

where I
catal is the catalytic current in the presence of Vitamin C, I
d the limiting current in the absence of Vitamin C and λ = kC
m
t (k, C
m and t are the catalytic rate constant, bulk concentration of Vitamin C and the elapsed time, respectively) is the argument of the error function. For λ >1.5, erf (λ 1/2) almost equals unity and Eq. (3) reduces to:
(4)
I
cata l
I d
=
λ
1 / 2
π
1 / 2
=
π
1 / 2
k
C m
t
1 / 2

From the slope of the I
catal /I
d plot the value of k at a given concentration of Vitamin C can be derived (Fig. 6C). The mean value of k in the concentration range of 0.01–0.5mM Vitamin C was found to be 2.5×10+5 cmmol−1 s−1.

Another advantage of the proposed modified electrode was that the resulting modified electrode showed good long term stability. Stability of the proposed electrode was tested by measuring the decrease in voltammetric current during repetitive SWV measurements of Vitamin C with β-CD/GQD/GCE stored in solution or air. For example, this electrode, within 24h, is used for the determination of 1mM Vitamin C in buffer solutions. Obtained results show that this electrode has remarkable stability without significant change in the voltammetric currents. When the electrode was stored in the atmosphere, the current response remained almost unchanged for about 1month (exactly 27days). RSD of repeated peak currents was (2.2%). The high stability of the β-CD/GQD/GCE could be related to the strong affinity of β-CD for GQD and the low solubility of the β-CD/GO-SO3H in water. To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording SWVs in buffer solution, containing 1mM of Vitamin C. The relative standard deviation was 3.44%. Therefore, β-CD/GQD/GCE imparts a suitable stability onto SWVs measurements of Vitamin C.

Following a qualitative assessment prepared electrodes GQD-GCE and GQD-β-CD-GCE, the electrochemical behavior of these sensors was investigated in further detail. Fig. 7 compares the cyclic voltammetric response of the GQD-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE. Two physiologically-relevant electroactive compounds were studied: dopamine and uric acid. The GQD-β-CD-GCE transfer electrodes display welled fined oxidation peaks for these analytes which detection of these compounds has interference with Vitamin C. Table 1 compares the peak potentials (Ep), and peak currents (Ip) for the six analytes employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of uric acid (UA) at GQD-β-CD-GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid is a common constituent in physiological fluids and a crucial component of the purine metabolism. It forms ions and salts known as urates and acid urates, such as ammonium acid urate. Uric acid is a product of the metabolic breakdown of purine nucleotides. High blood concentrations of uric acid can lead to gout and are associated with other medical conditions including diabetes and the formation of ammonium acid urate kidney stones. Therefore detection of UA is important in medicine [21].

In order to demonstrate the versatility of the fabrication methodology GQD-β-CD-GCE were applied to the detection of UA in physiological pH which was compare with unmodified GQD based electrode. Fig. 7A displays CVs for UA on the GQD-β-CD-GCE along with GQD-GCE. As can be seen, using GQD-β-CD-GCE, electrooxidation signal of UA was increased and oxidation potential was decreased to 0.26V. From these results it's found that, β-CD is suitable candidate for post modification of GQD towards detection of UA. Uric acid is a diprotic acid with pKa1 =5.4 and pKa2 =10.3. Thus in physiological pH (7.4), it forms the singly charged hydrogen or acid urate ion. As its second ionization is so weak, the full urate salts tend to hydrolyze.

The significant enhancement of the UA electrooxidation at GQD-β-CD-GCE can be explained according to the catalysis mechanism. Because the GQD and β-CD had a high surface area and functional group, respectively, GQD-β-CD-GCE boosted the faradic currents of the sluggish reaction as well as increased the amount of chemisorbed layer of the UA. This behavior catalyzed the electron transfer of UA in the solution. The more active functional groups on the surface of the GQD and β-CD can easily form hydrogen bonds with the amine groups of the UA, which weaken the NR bond energies; the electrons would be transferred through OH⋯NH. In addition, the density of the electron follows as UA>intermediate>product and therefore, intermediate and product electroactivity decreased and they were difficultly oxidized which is in accordance with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA) at GQD-β-CD-GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine is an organic chemical of the catecholamine and phenethylamine families that plays several important roles in the brain and body. Its name is derived from its chemical structure: it is an amine synthesized by removing a carboxyl group from a molecule of its precursor chemical L-DOPA, which is synthesized in the brain and kidneys. Dopamine is also synthesized in plants and most multicellular animals. In the brain, dopamine functions as a neurotransmitter-a chemical released by neurons (nerve cells) to send signals to other nerve cells. The brain includes several distinct dopamine pathways, one of which plays a major role in reward-motivated behavior. Most types of reward increase the level of dopamine in the brain, and most addictive drugs increase dopamine neuronal activity. It has been reported that DA undergoes a protective reaction affecting the fast chemical repair of oxidative free-radical damage to DNA [22]. It is suggested that the fall of levels of DA caused Parkinson's disease [23].

Various commonly usable analytical methods for DA and its analogs have been developed in the past. Some examples of these methods are rapid liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [24], the chromatography method [25], and the capillary electrophoresis mass spectrometry method [26]. These methods are very sensitive. Using this methods nanomolar concentrations of DA can be detected in real samples. All these techniques, however, require a compressing system, temperature controlling systems, separation systems, and other spectrophotometric or electric detection systems. Each technique generally suffers from some disadvantages with regard to cost and selectivity, the use of organic solvents, complex sample preparation procedures and long analysis time.

Electrochemical methods include advantages of low cost, high sensitivity and short measurement time. In the recent years, there is considerable interest in developing electrochemical methods for measurement of DA [27–31]. However, to the based our knowledge, none research group reported application of GQD based sensors on the detection of DA. Therefore, in the present work, GQDs based electrodes were used for the electrooxidation and detection of DA and found to be a highly active electrocalyst towards the electrooxidation of DA.

Cyclic voltammetry was used for investigation electrocatalytical activity of the GQD-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE for detection of DA. The electrochemical experiments in the presence of DA were carried out using modified and non-modified GQD-GCE. Fig. 7B shows CVs of bare GQD-β-CD-GCE and GQD-GCE in the presence of DA in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution with physiological pH (pH7.4). For GQD-GCE, it is found that the one redox peak with Ipox =8μA of DA are observed (Fig. 7B), although the peak current is remarkably increased at the GQD-β-CD-GCE due to the excellent electrical conductivity of β-CD. The Ipox for GQD-β-CD-GCE is 33.5μA, which is enough to distinguish the lower concentrations of DA. From the above figure, it can be concluded that without active substance, GQDs-GCE cannot result in the successful electrooxidation of DA. Also, when β-CD containing catalyst agent is used to modify GQD-GCE, the oxidation potential is obviously shifted to positive potentials which are disadvantages of proposed sensors. However, enlargement of current to 33.5μA using GQD-β-CD-GCE, suggesting that GQD-β-CD can provide acceleration centers to electron shuttling. Compartment the obtained CVs illustrated that; β-CD has more effective role on electrocatalytical behavior of prepared GQD based sensors towards detection of DA.

Finally, it is recognized that functional groups post modification elements of GQD can change the oxidation potential of analytes. The direct correlation of functional groups of analytes and post modification elements of GQD with peak potential values are presented in Table 2 . Therefore, change on the oxidation potential of the CV, resulting from the electrochemical interaction of reactant (analytes) with surface of modified GQDs, can be extremely useful for providing reliable chemical information about electroactivity of analyte. Also, it can be seen that amino acids with the same functional groups have similar oxidation potentials. Also, compartment of peak currents show that selected analytes has greater interaction with β-CD-GQD-GCE. Hence higher peak current of analytes is expected because of existence of accessible OH groups at the surface of β-CD. Therefore, the reason of this phenomenon is difference of functional groups of β-CD in GQD based electrodes.

In order to develop a voltammetric method for determining of Vitamin C, we selected the SWV mode, because the peaks are sharper and better defined at lower concentrations of DOX than those obtained by CV, with a lower background current, resulting in improved resolution. According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of Vitamin C. As mentioned previously, the PBS (pH7.4) was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of Vitamin C. The linear range for the determination of Vitamin C in human serum samples was investigated under optimal conditions. The linear range for spiked human serum samples were 0.01–170μM. In addition LOD values were 4.9×10−9 M, respectively. The detection and quantification limits calculated from calibration graphs, are also given in Table 3 . Fig. 8 illustrates the SWVs for determination of Vitamin C samples.

A comparison of the analytical performances of the previously reported methods and the proposed method for the determination of Vitamin C is summarized in Table 4 . It can be seen that the GQD-β-CD-GCE offered reasonable linear range for Vitamin C detection and the detection limit was lower than some of previous reports. These results indicated that GQD-β-CD-GCE is an appropriate platform for the determination of Vitamin C. On the other word, the prepared electrode shows voltammetric responses with low detection limit and wide linear range for Vitamin C in optimal conditions, which makes it suitable for determination of this physiologically-relevant electroactive compound.

Based on FDA validation guidelines, evaluation of the inter-day precision of the reported method were done by using the three concentrations of Vitamin C in 7days, also, the intraday evaluation of the methods was tested. For intra-day and inter-day precision, 5 replicated analyses of plasma and urine solutions with the Vitamin C concentrations of 0.1, 1.0 and 10μM were performed. Accuracy, percent of deviation between the true and measured values, and recovery, percent of the obtained value to real value of the sample, were also calculated. Tables 5 and 6
, are collected the inter-day and intra-day precision, accuracy and recovery of the method in urine and plasma samples. Regarding the maximum Food and Drug Administration (FDA) allowed coefficient of variation value of 15%, the method has favor accuracy and precision.

These data points revealed that the described method had an accepted degree of precision. Recoveries varied from 87.5 to 111.0% by comparing the calculated content with the real values of the added Vitamin C. The results showed that the suggested method was feasible for the analysis of Vitamin C in human plasma and urine samples.

The β-CD-GQD film was formed electrochemically in a regime of cyclic voltammetry on a glassy carbon electrode and checked for electrooxidation of Vitamin C in physiological pH (7.40). It is concluded that the electrooxidation of Vitamin C that starts around 0.01mV vs. Ag/AgCl occurs through a mediated electron transfer mechanism catalyzed by β-CD-GQD species which form in on the surface of GCE. The results show that the β-CD-GQD-GCE exhibit high electrocatalytic activity towards Vitamin C oxidation at around 0.15V. Therefore, β-CD-GQD can promote Vitamin C electron-transfer, thus β-CD-GQD/GCE provides higher electroactive surface area and lower charger transfer resistance compared with GQD-GCE. Using cyclic Voltammetry and chronoamperometry measurements, the kinetic parameters, such as charge transfer coefficient (α) and the catalytically reaction rate constant (k) for oxidation of Vitamin C were determined. It is expected that this work opens new horizons on the design of new nano-catalysts for the electrooxidation studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by the Nano Technology Research Center, and Faculty of Chemistry, Urmia University and Drug Applied Research Center Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.
